TAMSIN OXFORD - 27 JANUARY 2020

Connectivity may have evolved, but investment in new infrastructure shouldn't have to come at a heavy price.

According to Forrester, connectivity is the key enabler for agile services and customer-centric products and solutions. It's a catalyst for innovation, an essential component of the infrastructure required to benefit from subscription-based models, and sits at the core of the organisation's ability to innovate and pivot. This view is shared by Deloitte – the company points out that connectivity can 'drive development of new products and services, transform inefficient operating models, and make digital transformation possible'.

As subscription-based solutions such as Infrastructure-as-a-Service, Platform-as- a-Service and Everything-as-a-Service continue to gain traction alongside deeper investment into public and hybrid cloud, connectivity has become the bedrock of commercial success. This is further driven by the rise of emergent technologies such as artificial intelligence, machine learning, and the Internet of Things that have seeped steadily into infrastructure and innovation, providing inventive solutions to many persistent business problems. Automation is also gaining momentum – its ubiquity and cost-saving capabilities have already been proven in multiple operating environments. Each one of these solutions relies heavily on a reliable, transparent and powerful connectivity infrastructure to deliver results.

The Computer Weekly/TechTarget ‘IT Priorities Survey 2019’ found that network performance, network traffic and flow data were the key IT environmental factors under scrutiny by the IT buyer. Nearly half of respondents put upgrading their networking foundation at the top of their priority list. The business can no longer afford the luxury of being disconnected from the rest of the world – high-speed, reliable connectivity underpins growth and market reach.

The challenge facing the organisation as it moves away from the traditional weight of the MPLS network is not the availability of technology – SD-WAN has shifted gears considerably over the past two years – but rather the costs associated with its implementation. New technologies come with a price tag, one that rises the closer the solution comes to the so-called bleeding edge of innovation. It has to deliver return on investment that’s not at some far-off point in the financial future. It also has to consider the cost implications of remote offices, long distances, bandwidth, service provider and vendor, each one contributing a different layer to the budget and overall scope. Creep is easy; managing that creep, not so much.

Networking in the past was expensive. Today, SD-WAN is changing that, but not completely. Solutions can be negotiated, costs can be challenged in a competitive market, and the quality has improved dramatically. But there remains the risk of poorly optimised and badly specified implementations that end up being more expensive than the original network. To fully benefit from this new-found lease on connectivity life, without handing the financial benefit to someone else, the business has to work with reliable and capable last-mile connectivity partners, invest in well-managed and integrated support resources for ICT, and ensure ongoing visibility into all ICT expenditure.

Driving down the connectivity cost barrier

Strategic investment in a high-end connectivity solution was carefully managed to minimise expenditure and improve operational efficiency.

DMC, a market-leading South African debt collection company, had to invest in a new connectivity solution to drive costs down while increasing throughput. Re-negotiating the MPLS solution already in place had yielded minimal savings and didn’t provide the visibility and control that the company wanted. DMC had to consider alternative solutions that allowed for it to not only cut costs, but gain deeper control of its connectivity and the freedom to choose between multiple vendors.

“When we first decided to move away from MPLS, we wanted to see what solutions were available and went through all the major market players,” says Morné Owen, CIO, DMC. “We asked them about the possibility of an SDWAN solution and what they had to off er, but most were still busy looking at the technology and hadn’t yet come to terms with how they were planning to implement it.”

The vague answers did little to bolster the company’s confidence so Owen and his team moved from the service provider to the suppliers of SD-WAN solutions in the market in search of a solid solution that met their connectivity cost, visibility and control requirements. The process started out in 2017, and SD-WAN was a relatively new technology to the market, which meant that few companies understood its value or its implementation. To overcome this challenge, DMC partnered with CommsCloud, a managed IT infrastructure company, as it had the expertise and understanding required.

“We decided to investigate SD-WAN solutions back when there was a lot of talk about the technology, but not many implementations,” says Owen. “CommsCloud had helped us to reduce costs on a previous project and the team there had already established themselves as a trusted provider, so we leveraged their experience to find a solution that met our needs. At the time, we didn’t realise that we were sitting on the bleeding edge of the technology and this edge usually means that costs can spiral out of control. Fortunately, our ability to negotiate the best deal meant that this didn’t happen.”

The solution finally chosen for the project came from the Cisco stable of SD-WAN solutions – Cisco Meraki SD-WAN. The biggest switch bought by DMC for its SD-WAN platform was released the month the company started the implementation process – November 2017. Says Owen: “It was a bit more bleeding-edge than I would have liked, but the kit has proved itself.

“The biggest benefit of this solution is that we have end-to-end visibility into our lines,” he adds. “We like the control; we like being able to see where things break. The way in which the SD-WAN has been configured means that we can see exactly how traffic flows and this has opened up a whole new world. This fluidity and flexibility has eased our operational costs and reduced expensive downtime.”

The implementation refined how the company approaches large-scale implementations. DMC has walked away with a fully operational SD-WAN solution that has cut costs, improved connectivity and transformed operations.

“With this solution, we were also able to choose our connectivity suppliers and negotiate for the best possible price on our connectivity,” says Owen. “We had a saving in year one of 30% off our previous system. Coupled with that, we have a fully redundant solution with more control and visibility than ever before. Our connectivity costs, overall, have come down by 35% and our bandwidth has increased by 100% to 150% and delivered increased resiliency thanks to our multi-vendor approach.”

Manage connectivity expenditure, like a boss

Brainstorm: How can the business invest into a capable connectivity platform without breaking the bank?

Peter Walsh, MD, CommsCloud: Maximise the existing operational expenditure and optimise existing costs. This will free up cashflow for mission-critical projects. Then dump your MPLS VPN and migrate to SD-WAN and minimise the number of vendors you need to support without compromising your competitive edge.

Greg de Chasteauneuf, CTO, Saicom: Without a doubt, moving to SD-WAN as it’s ideal for connecting multiple branches into a single network dynamically and securely, and more cost-effectively than traditional networking solutions.

Hein Witte, specialised sales executive for Telecommunication Services, TSSA: The migration to a modern network and the operational costs of such a network over a period of time can be contracted in such a way that it’s completely predictable and flexible to accommodate business changes.

Neil Buckley, MD, Apex Business Intelligence: Be sure to negotiate. It’s a competitive market and there’s always wriggle room.

Brainstorm: What are some of the biggest costs associated with connectivity infrastructure today and which are the most important to ensure relevance and sustainability?

Michele McCann, head of Interconnection and Peering, Teraco: Terrestrial fibre costs are still high compared to Europe and Asia; however, it’s important to ensure that connectivity to the cloud, third parties and ISPs is always reliable. Fibre is here to stay.

Brian Timperley, MD and co-founder, Turrito Networks: The biggest costs are typically having a network over-architected, which happens when the service provider takes little interest in the customer’s actual requirements.

Peter Walsh, CommsCloud: Bad planning costs clients money all the time; so, too, does not managing your ISP, WAN provider, VoIP vendor, and PBX service provider billing and associated contracts effectively as these are the single biggest reasons why costs escalate in the business today.

Hein Witte, TSSA: Current corporate connectivity is costly due to the hierarchical design of a typical corporate network where all network traffic has to traverse the entire network to reach centralised systems and internet breakout.

Sean McCormick, MD, Globalstar Africa: Infrastructure, staff costs and goods available are resources that would contribute to the organisation’s bigger costs. You need to have good people who are productive and efficient in what they’re doing to remain sustainable in your business.

Neil Buckley, Apex Business Intelligence: The supplementary or auxiliary services applied, in addition to the core connectivity. Examples include SLA fees, security services, hardware rentals, failover and redundancy.

Douglas Turner, senior manager, Broad- Vision Technologies: Security. You’ll need to purchase appliances that have annual subscriptions attached, licensing, firewall units, intrusion detection, load balances.

Anthony Laing, GM of Networking, NEC XON: SD-WAN can offer you a good deal, but hidden costs such as licence, throughput, and other fees in some cases have actually ended up making these SD-WAN services more expensive than the ones they replaced.”

Brainstorm: What would you consider to be best practice in terms of managing connectivity expenditure?

Peter Walsh, CommsCloud: Understand your business need and align it with where the C-suite is taking the business – this is key to IT delivering solutions that work for end-users.

Hein Witte, TSSA: Understand current and future network budgets, traffic flows and capacity in demand, network user expectations and security requirements.

Greg de Chasteauneuf, Saicom: Work with a network-agnostic service provider to ensure you get network diversity and maximum uptime. Don’t sign long-term contracts; they lock you into a single connectivity solution that doesn’t provide for the changes in your business over the duration of the contract.

Brian Timperley, Turrito Networks: The ability to monitor a network’s performance, usage and data trends is critical to ensuring you’re getting the most from your spend and that your connectivity is correctly sized and specified to perform for your business.

Sean McCormick, Globalstar Africa: Find a good balance between cost and function – cheap isn’t always better. And ensure you give your resources proper training and skills so they can perform with confidence.

Michele McCann, Teraco: A clear interconnection strategy that provides direct interconnection between the enterprise network to cloud operators, ISPs and content providers. This will de-risk current and future strategies.

Neil Buckley, Apex Business Intelligence: Have a baseline benchmark and starting point from which to measure your future costs and consumption.

title: Who you gonna call for ICT?
author: Joanne
excerpt: MATTHEW BURBIDGE – 18 MARCH 2019 Managing telecommunications costs can be a tricky business. And that’s before you’re even getting started with IoT. Peter Walsh has started seven businesses, and his latest – CommsCloud – is, as he says, a ‘bespoke telecoms management consultancy’. Telecoms expense management is a crowded space, which is why he’s trying to do something different. And what with the coming wave of billions of connected sensors, he thinks he’s well placed to take advantage of this increasingly complex world. He says he tended to start businesses ‘because I stumble into them’. So he’s a serial entrepreneur? […]
content_text: MATTHEW BURBIDGE - 18 MARCH 2019 Managing telecommunications costs can be a tricky business. And that’s before you’re even getting started with IoT. Peter Walsh has started seven businesses, and his latest – CommsCloud – is, as he says, a ‘bespoke telecoms management consultancy’. Telecoms expense management is a crowded space, which is why he’s trying to do something different. And what with the coming wave of billions of connected sensors, he thinks he’s well placed to take advantage of this increasingly complex world. He says he tended to start businesses ‘because I stumble into them’. [{"id":"f3f24c4","elType":"section","settings":{"pa_condition_repeater":[]},"elements":[{"id":"779b19a6","elType":"column","settings":{"_column_size":100,"pa_condition_repeater":[]},"elements":[{"id":"f1b7bb3","elType":"widget","settings":{"editor":"

MATTHEW BURBIDGE - 18 MARCH 2019

Managing telecommunications costs can be a tricky business. And that’s before you’re even getting started with IoT.

Peter Walsh has started seven businesses, and his latest – CommsCloud – is, as he says, a ‘bespoke telecoms management consultancy’.

Telecoms expense management is a crowded space, which is why he’s trying to do something different. And what with the coming wave of billions of connected sensors, he thinks he’s well placed to take advantage of this increasingly complex world.

He says he tended to start businesses ‘because I stumble into them’.

So he’s a serial entrepreneur?

“I’m unemployable,” he says, laughing.

“I’m a maverick, but in my more mature years, I’ve learnt to control my blue sky (thinking).

“I’ve always got a new idea. And it’s very difficult to focus on building a business when you’ve always got a new idea. There comes a point where you have to be very pragmatic about what you’re doing.”

There’s a youthful energy about him, which belies his 59 years. He and his wife have just adopted a little girl, and he shows me a picture on his phone.

“If you don’t stay young, you’re going to be in big trouble.”

How did he fall into his latest business?

He returned to Cape Town in 1997, after ‘ten years of surfing in Jeffrey’s Bay’, and was casting around for something to do. He started a platform delivering prepaid airtime through interactive voice response, and was soon managing the distribution of airtime for trucking firms and enterprises, among others. Soon, he was also managing their post-paid contracts and Telkom lines.

This business was focussed solely on telecoms expense management, but by 2010, he says, ‘the model was broken’. He left, and started CommsCloud in January 2011, with one clear idea, which seems to have become something of a mantra: managing your business and managing your costs is a partnership.

Visibility into costs

One way of doing this is sitting down with the customer and the telco supplier and having a conversation about how to better manage costs. But part of the problem is that, in his view, large telcos are still struggling, and failing, to deliver proper services and value to enterprises.

“From a mobility perspective, they’re broken. Most enterprises have a mix of cellphones, and Vodacom and MTN are not vendor-agnostic. So Vodacom has its own management and reporting tools, which are designed to manage Vodacom. You need a vendor-agnostic platform. And you need a platform that isn’t marking its own homework.”

He’s got plenty of stories about inefficiencies he’s covered in large enterprises, and recounts one about how he was retained by a bank to analyse the data usage of its dongles. He said that despite the discount it received from the mobile operator, it was still spending R3.5 million a month, of which R1.8 million worth of data wasn’t being used.

“The point is they didn’t have visibility. The vendors can’t manage these cellphones; it’s not in their interest. Why would they cannibalise their own revenue stream?”

Telcos are also being squeezed. The price of voice telephony continues to drop, and will, he says, soon be free. Five years ago, a Diginet line cost thousands of rands a month. Now, fibre costs a fraction of that, with ten times the throughput, he says.

This also means that if the telecommunications costs continue to spiral, there will be precious little left to manage.

But we’re not quite at that point just yet. He bats my questions aside as to why the IT department can’t manage these costs.

“They can’t. It’s too big, and they can’t do it manually. They need a platform. You need to know what you’re doing; you need training and analytics skills, and you need time. And then add fixed line and VOIP calls…and what about cloud, what about inventory management, procurement lifecycle management?”

He pulls up a dashboard on his laptop, and starts clicking around a customer’s account. It’s comprehensive: voice, line rentals, internet usage, hosting, data, and discounts, among other things.

On the mobile dashboard, there’s a huge spike, for some reason, in March: it turns out someone from the company was travelling overseas that month, and picked up a lot of roaming costs.

The IoT opportunity

He says a typical c lient would, after six months, be saving anything from 25% to 40% on their telco bills, and this is all due to getting visibility into their usage and costs.

Does he see his business model changing, given the coming wave of disruptive technologies?

For Walsh, IoT represents a vast opportunity.

“If you haven’t got a platform that can measure all those IoT devices, and tie this back into the digital world with AI, you can’t help the company manage its ICT costs. For me, it was adapt or die.”

He says his platform is ready to manage all these devices, ‘in their tens of millions’.

He adds he has a global Access Point Name gateway for IoT devices.

He believes companies currently putting down sensors in Africa are going to be paying between R17 to R35, and perhaps even as high as R200, per megabyte of data.

“And that’s before you rent the SIM card and before you have a management platform.

“I can give IoT companies a SIM card with 5MB of data, which an IoT platform will never use, and a management tool for R5 a month, anywhere in the world. If you ask people what’s your AI strategy, what’s your IoT strategy, people don’t have them yet.

“If you have a billion devices out there, you can’t have a SIM that’s going to cost you R17 a month. You’ve got to manage them for R5 a month.

“And you’ve got to have a dashboard that enables you to link into the information and bring it back to you. The minute you have to send someone to the device to repair it, it’s going to cost you $700.”

Walsh seems to be getting in on the ground floor.

“When you get to the digital world, it requires a whole different set of tools, scaled for IoT, speed and automation and integration.

“I believe I’m enabling functionality with information and analytics, and I can only do that with AI and BI tools, and I can only do that if I’m saving somebody money, because everyone’s budget’s shot.”

Published on Brainstorm Magazine

May 19, 2014

Retail motor group CMH has cut its telecoms costs and increased its network capacity fourfold by migrating to a converged MPLS VPN network.Like most local companies, CMH was running separate voice and data networks. Telkom line infrastructure was in place for voice, and costs were being managed by utilising cell phone-based least-cost routing (LCR).

The Group was running point-to-point diginet links into its car dealerships and car rental offices countrywide, something that was proving both expensive and inefficient. There was also limited redundancy. If a line went down the dealership or office concerned was offline until someone could get a physical 3G dongle to the location or the line was repaired.

In October 2011, Group IT manager Roelof Minnaar met with Peter Walsh of CommsCloud, a specialist telecommunications consultancy.

Whilst initially the discussions were about gaining clarity on an array of VoIP proposals from multiple service providers (aimed at driving down costs), in the end Minnaar engaged CommsCloud to assist with analysing the Group’s telecommunications usage and costs, document its business need, and put together a request for proposal (RFP) for a tailor-made converged network.

It soon became clear to all the role players that converging the disparate voice and data networks would aggregate costs, increase savings, reduce operational impact and deliver significant benefits to the business.

Says Minnaar: “We drafted an RFP for a fully converged network servicing 54 dealerships and about 42 car rental depots, and distributed the RFP to the major players in the market. Thereafter we started a lengthy process of interacting with the respondents and working through the thick piles of RFPs they’d come back to us with.”

Minnaar says CommsCloud created a massive comparable matrix, analysing and plotting all the information in each RFP – from last mile redundancy to technical details, hardware and costs.

The service provider list was then trimmed down to three potential providers, with CMH conducting two site visits to the short-listed service providers’ support centres, gaining a better understanding of how CMH would be looked after post implementation.

Assessments concluded, CMH went with Vox Telecom because it felt it would get dedicated attention. A three-year deal was signed and planning was done. Each site was analysed to establish what connections it had and what it needed.

“The whole purpose was to ensure redundancy, increase throughput for the end-user and have a much better, more stable network. By converging the voice and data network we could ultimately afford a far better network,” Minnaar comments.

The decision to converge its network saved CMH almost R20m over the three-year contract period, of which half was reinvested back into last mile connectivity at its sites. The main links were sized based on specific needs and segregated to carry voice, video and data traffic. In addition, the secondary links were set up to be used by CMH IT to manage its own network, leaving the primary links for mission critical business applications.

CMH has also removed its least cost routers and significantly reduced the cost of calls. Voice traffic (VoIP) now runs over the converged network, at a lower cost, with inter-branch calls being routed at a negligible rate.

With careful planning, CommsCloud’s analysis and input and the willingness of its service provider to go the extra mile, CMH has been able to optimise its telecoms infrastructure, which has contributed significantly to on-going savings.

One spin off of the move has been to enable Minnaar to centralise the Group’s IT systems in its service provider’s datacentre. This is not only ensuring mission critical applications reside in a secured, controlled environment with generators and a replicated site allowing for immediate failover, but also ensures there is no single point of failure in accessing CMH’s business applications.

“CMH has almost completed its rollout,” says Minnaar. “And whilst it was not all smooth sailing, redundancy is now in place, throughput to the desktop has improved and the on-going demand from dealerships for more bandwidth is manageable”.

CMH’s network now services 78 sites housing 110 branches countrywide. It is scalable, provides alternative routing, and enables the Group to leverage technology for its businesses requirements. CMH is now free to focus on improving the customer experience and increasing productivity.

Originally posted on IT-ONLINE

eUICC for IoT sim cards (published by floLIVE) 

eUICC transforms IoT connectivity by allowing remote provisioning and seamless switching of SIM profiles, lowering costs and enhancing security for global IoT deployment. Explore the advantages of improved scalability and flexibility in IoT device management with Commscloud's Cloud Connect SIM Card's - powered by floLIVE.

As the world becomes increasingly connected and reliant on the internet, businesses must find new ways to stay ahead of the competition. One of the most promising new technologies in this regard is eUICC or Embedded Universal Integrated Circuit Card.

At its core, eUICC is a next-generation SIM card that provides businesses greater flexibility, security, and convenience than traditional SIM cards. With eUICC, companies can remotely provision and manage SIM cards, which allows them to easily switch between different networks and service providers without needing to replace SIM cards physically.

In this article, we'll explore the key features and benefits of eUICC capability on SIM cards and how businesses can leverage this technology to stay ahead of the competition.

Features of eUICC Capability on SIM Cards

    • Remote Provisioning: One of the most significant advantages of eUICC technology is the ability to provision and manage SIM cards remotely. The solution allows businesses to easily switch between different networks and service providers without physically replacing SIM cards. This is an approach that can save businesses significant time and money, as they no longer need to wait for SIM cards to be shipped or manually replaced.
    • Enhanced Security: eUICC technology provides businesses with enhanced security features, such as the ability to encrypt data and authenticate devices. This helps protect sensitive information from cyber-attacks and unauthorized access, which can be especially important for businesses that operate in highly regulated industries.
    • Increased Flexibility: With eUICC technology, businesses can choose different service providers and networks based on their needs and requirements. This can help companies stay competitive by allowing them to easily switch between different providers based on pricing, quality of service, or other factors.
    • Improved User Experience: eUICC technology also improves user experience by allowing users to easily switch between networks and service providers without manually replacing SIM cards. The technology can save users significant time and effort, improving overall service satisfaction.

Benefits of eUICC Capability on SIM Cards

    • Cost Savings: Cost savings are one of the most significant benefits of eUICC technology. By remotely managing SIM cards, businesses can save money on shipping, logistics, and manual labor associated with SIM card replacement.
    • Improved Network Coverage: eUICC technology can also help businesses improve network coverage by allowing them to switch between different networks based on availability and quality of service. Network Coverage can be especially important for companies that operate in remote or hard-to-reach areas, where network coverage can be unreliable.
    • Enhanced Security: eUICC technology provides enhanced security features that help protect sensitive information from cyber-attacks and unauthorized access. This can be especially important for businesses that operate in highly regulated industries, where compliance and security are critical
    • Greater Flexibility: With eUICC technology, businesses can choose different service providers and networks based on their needs and requirements. This can help companies stay competitive by allowing them to easily switch between different providers based on pricing, quality of service, or other factors.
    • Better User Experience: eUICC technology provides a better user experience by allowing users to easily switch between networks and service providers without manually replacing SIM cards. This can save users significant time and effort and improve overall satisfaction with the service.

In conclusion, eUICC technology provides businesses with various powerful features and benefits that can help them stay ahead of the competition. By remotely managing SIM cards, companies can save time and money, improve network coverage, enhance security, and provide a better user experience for their customers.

As the world becomes increasingly connected and reliant on the internet, eUICC technology will continue to play a critical role in helping businesses stay competitive and succeed in the digital age.

What is eUICC in IoT Connectivity?

eUICC Overview

The eUICC (Embedded Universal Integrated Circuit Card) is changing IoT connectivity by allowing flexible, remote provisioning of IoT devices. Unlike traditional SIM cards, eSIM technology integrates subscriber profiles without needing a physical card swap. This improves the security and management of IoT devices, making it crucial in modern IoT device management.

What is eSIM Technology?

eSIM, or embedded SIM, uses eUICC for remote SIM provisioning. Carrier profiles can be managed and updated over-the-air, which simplifies IoT device provisioning and ensures smooth operation without physical intervention.

How does eUICC differ from traditional SIM cards?

Traditional SIM cards need manual insertion and swapping. eUICC supports dynamic profile switching and M2M communication, offering carrier independence and better IoT security. This flexibility is key for global IoT deployments where device consistency and security matter most.

Benefits of Adopting eUICC in IoT

How does eUICC simplify device management?

eUICC makes IoT device management easier by allowing remote updates and management. This cuts down logistical challenges and streamlines the supply chain with efficient over-the-air updates.

What cost savings can be achieved with eUICC?

Using eUICC brings significant cost savings. It reduces operational costs and optimises resource use, enabling businesses to implement unified IoT solutions that boost efficiency and performance.

How does eUICC support multi-operator networks?

eUICC enables multi-operator support through dynamic profile switching. This ensures carrier independence, enhances global reach, and promotes seamless IoT interoperability.

What are the security enhancements offered by eUICC?

eUICC provides strong security enhancements for IoT connections. It includes secure IoT connections and infrastructure, ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements. Improved security protocols protect sensitive data, making IoT systems more robust against cyber threats.

Commscloud brings these advanced eUICC solutions, ensuring your IoT deployments are future-ready and secure. Explore more to see how eUICC can transform your IoT strategy today.

Practical Applications of eUICC in IoT

Global IoT Deployment

eUICC technology changes global IoT deployment by enabling over-the-air updates and easy integration across borders. This innovation offers carrier independence, allowing IoT devices to connect with various service providers without needing physical SIM card changes. Industries like logistics, healthcare, and smart cities benefit from eUICC integration due to better IoT customer experience and greater efficiency.

How does eUICC facilitate global IoT deployment?

eUICC makes global IoT deployment simple by allowing devices to switch between different networks easily. Over-the-air updates keep devices connected and performing well, enhancing cross-border IoT operations. This technology is a big advantage for IoT service providers by lowering dependency on individual carriers and improving the overall customer experience.

Which industries can benefit from eUICC technology?

Many industries gain from eUICC technology:

  • Logistics: Better operations and improved fleet tracking.
  • Healthcare: Remote patient monitoring and secure data transmission.
  • Smart Cities: Better connectivity for infrastructure and public services.

These industries enjoy improved scalability, flexibility, and operational efficiency.

Enhanced IoT Scalability and Flexibility

IoT projects achieve more scalability and network flexibility with eUICC. The technology offers cost savings by providing seamless connectivity and efficient IoT operations. Dynamic profile switching and remote management capabilities enhance Machine-to-Machine (M2M) communication, ensuring a scalable IoT infrastructure.

How does eUICC improve scalability for IoT projects?

eUICC boosts IoT scalability by enabling remote management and dynamic profile switching. These features lower operational costs and simplify device management across various locations, ensuring seamless connectivity and efficient operations.

What flexibility does eUICC provide for IoT networks?

eUICC offers unmatched flexibility for IoT networks by allowing multiple network profiles on one device. This flexibility supports dynamic profile switching and efficient remote management, resulting in better IoT network operations and scalability.

Real-World Use Cases of eUICC

eUICC drives innovation in IoT across different sectors, streamlining logistics, securing connections, and optimising resource utilisation.

Examples of eUICC implementation in logistics

In logistics, eUICC enables smoother operations by providing secure IoT connections and simplifying the supply chain. The technology optimises resource utilisation, ensuring reliable and efficient logistics management.

How healthcare industry utilises eUICC for remote patient monitoring

The healthcare industry uses eUICC for remote patient monitoring, benefiting from secure IoT connections and optimised resource utilisation. This technology improves IoT hardware efficiency and drives innovation in patient care and monitoring systems.

For more insights on how eUICC can transform your IoT projects, visit Commscloud.

Future-Proofing IoT Deployments

In a world of constant tech changes, future-proofing IoT deployments is vital. Using eUICC technology keeps IoT solutions flexible and scalable. eUICC technology helps with easy lifecycle management and smart resource use, making IoT projects successful for the long haul.

Key Benefits:

  • Better flexibility
  • Easy IoT lifecycle management
  • Smart resource use

Using the latest in eUICC gives you an edge, keeping your IoT systems updated and competitive.

Improving IoT Operations with eUICC

For smooth IoT operations, good maintenance and management are key. With eUICC remote management, IoT service providers can boost efficiency and make logistics simpler. This leads to better global coverage and reliable service.

Operational Enhancements:

  • Remote eUICC management
  • Simpler logistics
  • Better global coverage

Adding eUICC to your IoT setup cuts down on operational issues, making IoT deployment smooth and efficient.

Choosing the Right eUICC Solution

Picking the right eUICC solution needs care. The market has many eUICC platforms and unified IoT options for different needs. Regulatory compliance and carrier freedom are big benefits of a strong eUICC ecosystem.

Factors to Consider:

  • Regulatory compliance
  • Carrier freedom
  • Complete eUICC solutions

Choose a provider with the right eUICC platform to improve your IoT strategy and stay ahead of the competition.

Key Insights

  • eUICC technology allows devices to easily swap SIM subscriber profiles over the air, promoting efficiency and flexibility in large-scale IoT deployments.
  • The advantage of eUICC is that it enables remote provisioning of carrier profiles on IoT devices, simplifying logistics and reducing operational costs.
  • eUICCs are beneficial for improving IoT infrastructure by enabling multi-operator support, which enhances network flexibility and scalability.
  • eUICC ensures secure connections and compliance with regulatory standards, making it suitable for applications like telematics, remote patient monitoring, and fleet management.
  • Adopting eUICC helps streamline the supply chain and IoT subscription management, improving customer experience and resource utilization.
  • Future-proofing IoT deployments with eUICC reduces vendor lock-ins and facilitates dynamic profile switching, enhancing global reach and interoperability.
  • We provide expert solutions integrating eUICC, offering comprehensive benefits for various industries such as automotive, retail, and public safety.

  • eUICC ensures secure connections and compliance with regulatory standards, making it suitable for applications like telematics, remote patient monitoring, and fleet management.
  • Adopting eUICC helps streamline the supply chain and IoT subscription management, improving customer experience and resource utilization.
  • Future-proofing IoT deployments with eUICC reduces vendor lock-ins and facilitates dynamic profile switching, enhancing global reach and interoperability.
  • We provide expert solutions integrating eUICC, offering comprehensive benefits for various industries such as automotive, retail, and public safety.

May 21 - 2023 | Vera Miretsky

Optimize your IoT solutions with effective network steering strategies, enhancing performance and connectivity in smart systems with Commscloud Africa.

The Essential Guide to Network Steering in IoT Connectivity

In the world of Internet of Things (IoT), ensuring consistent and reliable connectivity is paramount. This is where network steering comes into play, a crucial concept for businesses leveraging IoT technology. Unlike traditional consumer devices, IoT devices demand specialised strategies to maintain connectivity due to their heightened sensitivity to performance issues such as latency and service quality. Let's explore the nuances of network steering and how it optimises IoT operations.

Understanding Network Steering

Network steering is the process by which mobile operators direct IoT devices to connect to specific networks that best align with business goals. This involves prioritising networks based on commercial agreements rather than optimal coverage or performance. For typical mobile users, network choice can be manual, offering flexibility during travel. However, IoT devices lack this option, requiring automated, strategic network steering to avoid service disruptions that could have severe implications.

Three Pillars of IoT Network Steering

IoT devices can adopt three main approaches to network steering, each with its distinct advantages and challenges:

1. SIM-Based Steering:

   - Advantages: Directs devices to preferred networks, saving battery and reducing connection times.

   - Disadvantages: Fails to connect if no preferred network is available, which can be critical for essential services.

2. Core Network Steering:

   - Advantages: Offers flexible control, allowing devices to connect to the strongest network available.

   - Disadvantages: Can result in longer connection times, affecting devices with strict time constraints to establish a connection.

3. Billing-Based Steering:

   - Advantages: Enables real-time control over data usage and costs by monitoring and managing connections based on set quotas.

   - Disadvantages: May attempt to connect to blocked networks, leading to unnecessary battery drain and additional communication overhead.

Tailoring Steering to Your Needs

The choice of steering method should be dictated by specific business requirements:

- Efficiency and Battery Life: SIM-based steering is ideal for battery-dependent devices.

- Control: Core network steering offers comprehensive control for businesses needing robust oversight.

- Cost Management: Billing-based steering helps manage expenses through precise data usage monitoring.

Integrative Solutions by CommsCloud

Recognizing the diverse needs of IoT businesses, CommsCloud utilises our Connectivity Technology Partner floLIVE’s comprehensive solution that integrates all three steering methods into a single platform. This approach allows for customised configurations that align with specific business needs, ensuring optimised service quality and cost-efficiency. floLIVE's platform isn’t about limiting to certain networks but providing the best options tailored to each customer.

Conclusion

In the evolving landscape of IoT, effective network steering is more than just a technical necessity; it's a strategic asset. By understanding and implementing the right steering method, businesses can enhance the reliability and efficiency of their IoT operations. With an integrated platform, the power to optimise IoT connectivity is more accessible and impactful than ever. We ensure that IoT businesses not only meet their connectivity needs but also achieve a competitive edge in the IoT market, demonstrating the true power of partnership in the digital age.

CommsCloud IoT Connectivity Solutions | Empowering Your Business to Connect, Grow, and Thrive – Contact Us | +27 21 551 5526 or  Get in Touch and follow our journey across Africa with the social media links below.

What is IoT Steering?

IoT Steering means connecting Internet of Things (IoT) tech with steering systems. This makes a network of connected devices that improve steering control. With IoT-enabled steering, vehicles gain better performance, efficiency, and safety.

How does IoT Steering work?

IoT Steering uses IoT control systems and real-time steering tech. Sensors and connected devices collect data, which is processed and sent to ensure smooth IoT steering connectivity. This helps in making accurate steering changes based on driving conditions.

Why is IoT Steering important?

IoT Steering boosts efficiency and reliability in advanced steering systems. With IoT-driven steering enhancements, vehicles become more precise, improving safety and performance.

Key Components of IoT Steering

IoT Steering includes sensors, infrastructure, and IoT-based steering mechanisms. These work together, using innovative tech, to offer better steering functions.

What are the main elements of an IoT Steering system?

An IoT Steering system consists of:

  • Sensors: Gather data for steering decisions.
  • Infrastructure: Supports data processing and connectivity.
  • Intelligent Steering Technology: Uses IoT modules and software for better steering control.

How do sensors play a role in IoT Steering?

Sensors are key in IoT Steering. They collect data through wireless networks. This info is used for IoT steering diagnostics and telemetry, making the system more responsive and precise.

Benefits of IoT Steering

IoT Steering offers many benefits in terms of efficiency and management. It helps with IoT steering optimization and performance, leading to better operations.

How does IoT Steering enhance operational efficiency?

By using IoT steering automation, IoT Steering boosts operational efficiency. The tech supports smoother steering operations and helps in IoT predictive maintenance for better vehicle management.

What advantages does IoT Steering provide for fleet management?

IoT Steering offers several advantages for fleet management:

  • Better operational efficiency with IoT fleet steering.
  • Improved coordination with connected car systems.
  • Remote steering control via IoT for quicker responses and improved fleet management.

These benefits help businesses run their fleets more efficiently and safely.

Implementation of IoT Steering in Various Industries

How is IoT Steering used in the automotive industry?

In the automotive industry, IoT steering integration changed vehicle control and management. IoT automotive control systems improve connected car systems by using smart vehicle control and wireless sensors. This tech helps with predictive maintenance by checking vehicle health and fixing problems before they happen, leading to big changes in vehicle steering.

Key Benefits:

  • Better safety through real-time data monitoring.
  • Improved fuel efficiency with optimized driving patterns.
  • Proactive maintenance, cutting downtime and repair costs.

What are the applications of IoT Steering in smart cities?

In smart cities, IoT steering integration helps improve urban living. Network protocols and edge computing make fleet management and city services automation more efficient. This boosts operational efficiency and gives solid data analytics for making decisions.

Applications Include:

  • Efficient public transport management.
  • Smart traffic control systems.
  • Better waste management with automated routes.

IoT Steering and Security

What are the security challenges of IoT Steering?

IoT cybersecurity is crucial as more devices get connected. Challenges include securing cryptographic protocols, stopping cybercrime, and managing risk.

Security Challenges:

  • Data breaches due to weak encryption.
  • Higher vulnerability to cyber-attacks.
  • Tough threat prevention and real-time monitoring.

How can IoT Steering improve cybersecurity in connected devices?

IoT steering boosts cybersecurity by using advanced data analytics and real-time monitoring. Encryption and strong authentication methods improve cryptography, reducing threats and ensuring secure communications.

Security Enhancements:

  • Real-time monitoring for quick threat detection.
  • Encryption to protect data transfers.
  • Authentication to verify device integrity.

Future Trends in IoT Steering

The landscape of IoT steering technology is rapidly evolving, driven by big advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and algorithms. Emerging trends in IoT steering technology include:

  • Intelligent Steering Technology: Using AI for real-time adjustments and predictive maintenance.
  • IoT-Enabled Steering: Enhancing connectivity and data exchange between vehicles and infrastructure for better control.
  • Advanced Steering Systems: Integrating sensors and actuators for more responsive and accurate steering.

These innovations promise to redefine how we interact with steering mechanisms and improve overall vehicle safety and performance.

How will IoT Steering evolve with advancements in AI?

Advancements in AI are set to change IoT steering systems through:

  • Evolution of Algorithms: Improving the precision and reliability of steering functions.
  • Cybersecurity Measures: Adding strong protections against cyber threats targeting IoT devices.
  • Autonomous Steering IoT: Creating self-driving vehicles with advanced IoT steering capabilities.
  • IoT Steering Optimization: Using AI to fine-tune steering responsiveness and efficiency.

As AI continues to innovate, the evolution of IoT-driven steering enhancements and automation will lead to smarter, more autonomous vehicles.

Selecting the Right IoT Steering Solutions

Choosing the right IoT steering solutions means considering several factors:

  • Company Strategy: Aligning IoT steering integration with overall business goals.
  • Technology Compatibility: Making sure IoT steering software and connected devices work with existing systems.
  • Cybersecurity: Protecting IoT-based steering mechanisms from potential threats.

By developing a strategic approach, companies can effectively integrate IoT steering solutions within their IoT infrastructure.

How can businesses ensure compatibility with their existing IoT infrastructure?

To ensure smooth compatibility with existing IoT infrastructure, businesses should focus on:

  • Infrastructure Assessment: Checking current IoT systems to find compatibility needs.
  • Strategic Planning: Creating strategies that include IoT steering connectivity and IoT vehicle steering into existing frameworks.
  • Compliance and Standards: Following industry standards for IoT control systems and IoT steering network operations.

These steps help maintain operational efficiency and promote successful IoT steering integration.

CommsCloud - Driving Innovation in IoT Steering

CommsCloud leads in IoT steering technology innovations. The company's strategy focuses on creating advanced solutions that enhance IoT steering connectivity and integration. Key areas of CommsCloud's offerings include:

  • Intelligent Steering Technology: Advanced systems that improve steering accuracy and responsiveness.
  • IoT-Driven Steering Enhancement: Solutions that use IoT data for real-time improvements and predictive maintenance.

CommsCloud's commitment to innovation ensures that businesses have access to the most advanced IoT steering technologies available.

What are the unique features of CommsCloud's IoT Steering offerings?

CommsCloud offers a range of special features in its IoT steering solutions:

  • Advanced Steering Systems: Integrating state-of-the-art technology for superior performance.
  • IoT-Enabled Steering: Improving connectivity and real-time data exchange.
  • IoT Steering Software: Offering robust diagnostic and telemetry capabilities for proactive maintenance.

These features make CommsCloud a leader in the field, providing reliable and innovative IoT steering solutions tailored to modern business needs.

 

Key Insights

  • IoT Steering Applications: We offer detailed IoT-enabled steering solutions. These optimise vehicle performance with advanced IoT steering sensors and IoT steering automation. Our approach includes IoT predictive maintenance steering to ensure reliability and efficiency.
  • Asset and Fleet Management: Improve your fleet management with IoT steering tracking and connected steering devices. Our solutions integrate asset tracking and monitoring for better operational performance.
  • Strategic IoT Implementation: We help companies deploy IoT steering systems, offering insights on steering IoT implementation, network protocols, and remote steering control IoT for smooth integration.
  • Innovative Technologies: Use advanced technologies such as edge computing, autonomous steering IoT, and wireless steering IoT for better vehicle control and user experience. Our focus on innovation keeps you ahead of the competition.
  • Safety and Security: Focus on safety with IoT steering optimisation and IoT steering feedback mechanisms. We have strong IoT security measures to protect against cyber attacks, ensuring the safety and reliability of your steering systems.
  • Industry-Specific Solutions: Our IoT steering modules and software cater to various applications, from smart homes to fleet and asset management in sectors like food trucks and tourism.

author: Joanne
excerpt: IoT roaming limitations and restrictions lead to higher costs and complex regulations. Commscloud offers solutions for managing IoT connectivity issues and ensuring reliable global operation.
content_text: IoT roaming limitations and restrictions lead to higher costs and complex regulations. A variety of these IoT devices must work across borders, whether they are static devices like metering, POS (Point of Sale) systems, healthcare equipment, or mobile devices in motion like asset trackers or fleet management solutions. Why Roaming IoT SIM cards are Inadequate ?

IoT technologies have developed at breakneck speed in the past decade, much faster than the existing cellular infrastructure could keep up with. A variety of these IoT devices must work across borders, whether they are static devices like metering, POS (Point of Sale) systems, healthcare equipment, or mobile devices in motion like asset trackers or fleet management solutions. Many of these IoT devices come equipped with a roaming simcard provided by a mobile operator or other connectivity provider.

However, if we look at the very basics of it, the roaming infrastructure wasn’t built to support a large number of devices for an extended period of time. The idea of roaming was that a very small percentage of consumers would be roaming at any given time, such as tourists and business travelers, ensuring the network would never get overwhelmed.**

Inadequate roaming infrastructure, high roaming costs, and restrictive GDPR and other privacy regulations make roaming an ill-fitting solution for IoT technologies. Here we look at the five main reasons why roaming isn’t an adequate solution for connected devices and what companies should consider instead.

Poor Performance

First and foremost, let’s look at performance. Regardless of where the device is based, the data it generates is routed through the home network before reaching its destination. This leads to increased latency as it takes time for the data to travel to the home mobile operator and back. This slows down communications and can easily drain the battery, which in itself is a massive challenge for IoT solutions.

GDPR and Other Privacy Violations

Data privacy is becoming increasingly important, and privacy regulations can differ per country or sector, making it difficult for companies to stay on top of them. For example, you may have a POS device in Germany that uses a CloudConnect Simcard from a US mobile operator. This cross-border data transfer could violate GDPR rules, which require that data originating in the EU remain within the EU.

This is, of course, an oversimplified example. But imagine an enterprise customer with hundreds of devices deployed across 10 or 20 different countries. Staying on top of privacy regulations for each country and providing a seamless connectivity solution can become mission impossible.

Permanent Roaming Restrictions

The exponential growth of connected devices means that certain countries and independent mobile operators have applied their own “permanent roaming bans” to protect their connectivity bandwidth. In this case, devices can use roaming for a limited period of time, usually between three to six months, after which point they’re disconnected without the option to regain connectivity.

Some countries like Turkey, China, and Brazil have explicit bans on permanent roaming while others have a more subtle, yet equally limiting approach. The Middle East, for example, requires that all cellular connectivity services be provided by a licensed local operator, which effectively excludes the option for permanent roaming. In the U.S., Canada, and Australia, the onus is on the network operators to decide whether they allow permanent roaming or not, depending on the relationship they have with the connectivity provider.

High Roaming Costs

Roaming costs differ per country and per roaming agreement, so they can be incredibly hard to predict. While IoT-specific roaming agreements can bundle different data plans together to make them more affordable, you still run the risk of incurring additional charges or having these agreements change without much warning.

Imagine that your operations rely on large data transmissions. One simple change in the pricing structure can have a massive impact on your bottom line. Without the option to manage your connectivity over-the-air (OTA), changing providers can become a costly exercise in itself as you have to physically replace SIM cards in all of your devices.

In general, using roaming for data transmission would generally be more expensive than relying on a local network provider. Depending on the size of your operations, this can become an unsustainable way of running your business over time.

IoT Security

And last but not least, when we talk about roaming there are inherent security concerns. Not only do you not have visibility over how your data travels, but you also don’t have control over how the host Mobile Network Operator (MNO) secures its network. This means that you can’t have any assurances regarding security when you’re deploying your devices abroad.

This goes back to the overall discussion around infrastructure – using the existing roaming models leaves you at the mercy of the host MNO which you have no control over and which may observe lower security standards. This unwittingly puts you at a greater risk of having your devices compromised.

Moving Ahead with IoT 

It’s only natural that the first IoT solutions relied on the roaming services that were available at the time. It’s the best IoT providers could do with what was available to them.

However, we’re reaching a point where IoT solutions can no longer rely on traditional roaming agreements and a network infrastructure that’s been built for individual consumers. Instead, IoT technology providers need to look at alternatives which give them a better, more secure, and a more sustainable solution long term – built with IoT in mind. This means taking advantage of local connectivity, at local rates, on a global level.

If you want to find out how you can do this, then get in touch with our team at CommsCloud and we’d be happy to walk you through your options and find the best IoT connectivity solution for you.

CommsCloud IoT Connectivity Solutions | Empowering Your Business to Connect, Grow, and Thrive – Contact Us | +27 21 551 5526 or  Get in Touch and follow our journey across Africa with the social media links below.

Challenges in IoT Connectivity

What are the common connectivity issues with IoT devices?

IoT devices often have connectivity issues that hurt performance and reliability. Problems like weak signal strength, high latency, and inconsistent network availability are common IoT connectivity issues. There are also interoperability problems due to the diverse range of devices and communication protocols. IoT communication barriers can limit seamless data exchange. Reliable SIM cards and a strong network infrastructure are crucial to solve these issues.

Which regions face the most significant IoT roaming restrictions?

Some regions have strict IoT roaming restrictions due to different regulations and policies. Countries with tough regulatory frameworks may enforce significant IoT regional restrictions, impacting international usage. This makes it hard to maintain continuous connectivity when devices cross borders, affecting IoT solutions' effectiveness in global deployments.

How do different network agreements impact IoT device performance?

Network agreements significantly impact IoT device performance. The terms set by mobile networks and IoT service providers can greatly affect connectivity quality. IoT roaming agreements between carriers decide how well devices operate across various networks. These agreements' limitations and the nature of IoT carrier partnerships directly influence performance and service continuity.

Privacy and Compliance Issues

How does GDPR affect IoT data transfer?

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) has major implications for IoT data transfer across borders. GDPR mandates strict compliance requirements to ensure personal data's privacy and security. IoT devices must follow these regulations, leading to IoT data privacy concerns and constraints. Proper data management and processing are needed to avoid legal issues and secure IoT data transfer.

What are the privacy risks associated with IoT roaming?

Privacy risks in IoT roaming are a major concern. Managing IoT data privacy becomes complex as devices move across regions. Unauthorized access to sensitive information, data breaches, and IoT security risks are significant challenges. Effective IoT roaming data management strategies are essential to reduce these risks and protect user privacy.

Are there specific compliance requirements for IoT devices in foreign networks?

IoT devices must meet specific compliance requirements when operating in foreign networks. These vary by region and involve IoT cross-border challenges, such as following local data protection laws and ensuring compatibility with regional standards. Ensuring IoT international integration requires understanding and complying with these regulations to achieve seamless and lawful operations.

Cost Implications of IoT Roaming

What costs are involved in IoT roaming?

The cost implications of IoT roaming cover various aspects like roaming charges, IoT device expenses, and IoT roaming fees. Usually, IoT connectivity tariffs can greatly impact the total cost. Mobile IoT limitations also affect these costs, often leading to higher tariffs and extra roaming charges.

How do roaming fees vary across different markets?

Roaming fees for IoT devices differ widely across markets due to different regulations and IoT roaming restrictions. Each market's specific rules can affect IoT service provider limitations, influencing both accessibility and costs of IoT networks. Therefore, organisations must navigate these variations to manage their IoT expenses effectively.

What strategies can help manage high IoT roaming costs?

To manage high IoT roaming costs, several strategies can be applied:

  • Use advanced technology to optimise data transfer.
  • Implement IoT cost control measures.
  • Efficiently manage IoT devices to reduce unnecessary roaming.
  • Proactively monitor to prevent IoT service disruptions.

Security Concerns in IoT Roaming

What are the primary security risks for IoT devices on foreign networks?

IoT devices face many security risks when on foreign networks. These include cyber security threats, potential breaches, and other IoT security challenges. IoT data privacy concerns increase due to unfamiliar network environments.

How can IoT devices be protected while roaming internationally?

Protecting IoT devices while roaming involves several cybersecurity measures:

  • Employ strong IoT protection strategies.
  • Enhance mobile security to safeguard data.
  • Address IoT cross-border challenges with comprehensive security solutions.

What role do MNOs play in ensuring IoT security across borders?

Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) are vital in maintaining IoT security across borders. Their policies and regulations ensure robust security measures are in place. Collaborating through IoT carrier partnerships and IoT roaming agreements strengthens IoT security policies, reducing potential risks.

Commscloud is committed to providing solutions to handle the challenges and costs associated with IoT roaming, ensuring secure and efficient IoT connectivity across global markets.

Permanent Roaming Restrictions

What are permanent roaming restrictions and how do they affect IoT?

Permanent roaming restrictions are rules for international SIM cards, limiting their use in other countries. These rules can disrupt IoT devices, causing issues like downtime and compliance problems. Businesses need solid plans to keep IoT devices running smoothly, especially when used in different countries.

Which countries have the most stringent permanent roaming restrictions?

Some countries have tough permanent roaming restrictions:

  • China: Requires local network solutions for foreign SIM cards.
  • Turkey: Needs agreements with local operators for international roaming.
  • UAE: Limits extended use of foreign SIM cards.
  • Brazil: Has strict rules that affect cross-border IoT projects.

These regional restrictions make it hard to keep IoT services running across borders.

How can businesses navigate permanent roaming restrictions for IoT deployments?

To handle these restrictions, businesses should try these strategies:

  • Compliance Management: Follow local rules for IoT roaming.
  • Local Partnerships: Work with local network providers to avoid connectivity issues.
  • Flexible SIM Solutions: Use multi-IMSI or eSIM technology to switch networks easily.
  • Cost Control: Keep track of roaming costs to manage budgets better.

Using these strategies helps businesses overcome challenges and keep IoT operations efficient.

Future of IoT Connectivity

What future trends will impact IoT roaming?

The future of IoT connectivity will be influenced by:

  • 5G Networks: Providing faster and more reliable connections.
  • AI Integration: Making devices smarter and more efficient.
  • Edge Computing: Cutting down latency and speeding up data processing at the network's edge.

These trends will push IoT technology forward.

How is technology evolving to address IoT roaming limitations?

New tech is fixing IoT roaming issues:

  • eSIM Technology: Allows remote SIM updates, making devices more flexible.
  • Network Protocol Innovations: Improve data transmission and security.
  • Global Connectivity Platforms: Manage IoT devices across various regions seamlessly.

These advances are crucial for better IoT connectivity.

What role will eSIMs play in overcoming IoT roaming constraints?

eSIMs are a key innovation for IoT. They allow:

  • Enhanced Roaming Flexibility: Devices can switch networks without changing SIM cards.
  • Operational Efficiency: Easier management of large IoT deployments.
  • Future-Proof Solutions: Ready for evolving connectivity needs.

eSIMs help solve roaming problems and support global IoT connectivity.

Key Insights

  • IoT devices need to function indefinitely on foreign networks without returning, leading to increased costs and constraints.
  • Organizations with SIM cards roaming on foreign networks for long periods face issues like limited connectivity and network accessibility.
  • Permanent roaming of IoT devices can increase latency and create complex regulations that limit inbound permanent M2M roaming.
  • Devices with lifecycles of up to 10 years require solutions that enable permanent roaming without major constraints.
  • Different networks connect through predefined agreements between mobile operators to minimize IoT communication barriers.
  • High costs and roaming charges are significant concerns, along with interoperability problems and IoT SIM card restrictions.
  • Effective IoT solutions must address network selection, platform compatibility, and carrier partnerships to overcome operational hurdles and service disruptions.

iSIM Explained: What is an iSIM?. Learn the differences between traditional SIMs, eSIMs, and iSIMs. Discover how an integrated subscriber identity module simplifies IoT connectivity with Commscloud.

iSIM Explained - What is an iSim?

An integrated SIM (iSIM) is an alternative form factor to traditional SIM cards. Instead of physically placing a SIM card into a device, as with mobile phones or IoT devices, iSIMs are embedded directly onto the device's System-on-a-Chip (SOC). This eliminates the need for a removable SIM card and simplifies the device's design.

What's the Difference Between eSIMs and iSIMs?

While eSIMs are soldered to a device's circuit board, iSIM technology integrates the SIM into a Tamper-Resistant Element (TRE) on the device's SOC. This approach uses less power and can be activated wirelessly using a secure industry-accepted protocol, removing the need for specific SIM hardware.

How Does System-on-a-Chip (SOC) Work?

To understand iSIM, let's look at how it integrates with the device's SOC. Incorporating the SIM directly into the SOC reduces costs, simplifies manufacturing, and minimizes power consumption for battery-powered devices. This integration also saves space on printed circuit boards (PCBs) and reduces overall costs for enterprises and device manufacturers.

What are the Benefits of iSIM?

iSIM offers several advantages over traditional SIM technologies:

1. Size: iSIMs are small, under 1 millimeter squared, allowing for more compact device designs.
2. Security: iSIMs are integrated into the SOC's secure enclave, making them highly resistant to tampering and unauthorised access.
3. Resources: iSIMs use less power and reduce the need for multiple components, lowering manufacturing and logistical costs.
4. Compatibility: iSIMs can connect to various network types and allow devices to switch networks over the air (OTA) based on multiple factors.
5. Use Cases: iSIMs enable new business models and applications across various industries, from utility meters to medical wearables and vehicle telematics systems.

How Does CommsCloud Use iSIM?

At CommsCloud, we have chosen to work with floLIVE and their partners Kigen and Sony Semiconductors to use their unique IMSI library with iSIM. The solution allows enterprises to access compliant, multi-network local coverage for various IoT use cases. Our multi-IMSI technology using iSIM offers cost-effectiveness, minimal footprint, and robust security and flexibility. We support a range of SIM technologies and help enterprises manage and scale their global IoT projects.

Are you interested in learning more about iSIMs?

Feel free to reach out if you want to deepen your understanding of iSIM technology. To discover how iSIM can revolutionise your IoT connectivity and unlock the full potential of connected devices, contact us at +27 21 551 5526 or  Get in Touch. You can also follow our journey across Africa with the social media links below.

Integrated SIM (iSIM) Basics

What is an iSIM and how does it work?

An integrated SIM, or iSIM, is a new type of subscriber identity module that's built right into a device's hardware. Unlike traditional SIM cards that you can remove, or eSIMs that are soldered onto the motherboard, iSIM integrates the SIM functionality straight into the System-on-a-Chip (SoC). This means the integrated subscriber identity module is a part of the device's main processor.

Key Characteristics of iSIM Technology

  • iSIM Features: Small size, lower power use, and higher efficiency.
  • iSIM Security: Better security with hardware-level encryption.
  • iSIM Standards: Follows global telecom standards for compatibility.

Technical Architecture of iSIM

How is iSIM integrated into devices?

iSIMs are put into devices by embedding them in the system on a chip (SoC). This integration allows direct connection to mobile networks without needing a separate slot for physical SIM cards. The architecture of iSIM enables smooth connectivity and strong performance in various devices.

iSIM Functionality within System-on-a-Chip (SoC)

  • iSIM in Smartphones: Lets manufacturers save space and boost battery life.
  • iSIM in Wearables: Offers better design options and smaller hardware.
  • iSIM in IoT Devices: Enhances data management and network connections.

Security Features of iSIM

  • Encryption: Provides advanced encryption to keep user data safe.
  • iSIM Provisioning: Manages the activation and deactivation of network services securely.
  • Lifecycle Management: Ensures secure updates and efficient operation through the device's lifespan.

Advantages of Using iSIM in IoT Devices

Benefits of iSIM for IoT Applications

Integrated SIMs offer many benefits for IoT applications, like saving space, improving security, and better device lifecycle management. As iSIM trends grow, they will drive significant innovation and efficiency in IoT fields.

Efficiency and Cost Implications of iSIM

  • Cost Efficiency: Lower production costs due to fewer parts.
  • Performance: Better performance through efficient resource use.
  • Management: Makes managing many IoT devices easier.

Secure Connectivity with iSIM

  • Secure Connectivity: Ensures encrypted communication across networks.
  • Provisioning: Streamlined provisioning processes enhance flexibility.
  • Lifecycle Management: Complete management from deployment to end-of-life.

Commscloud aims to use iSIM technology for next-generation devices, ensuring secure, efficient, and flexible connectivity solutions.

Implementation and Use Cases

How is iSIM implemented in various industries?

iSIM in Healthcare

In healthcare, iSIM tech helps secure communication between IoT devices like medical sensors and smartphones. With iSIM, doctors can monitor patient data in real-time, improving care and outcomes.

iSIM for EV Charging Stations

iSIM is changing EV charging stations by ensuring safe and efficient connection between charging points and management systems. This makes charging stations more reliable for users.

Market Trends and Future Potential

What are the current and future trends in iSIM adoption?

Impact of iSIM on 5G Networks

Connecting iSIM with 5G networks is a big deal. iSIM gives better connection and security, key for spreading 5G. It will help set new network standards.

Future Prospects of iSIM in Consumer Electronics

iSIM is set to be big in consumer electronics like wearables and smartcards. Its ability to simplify connections and improve device functions means it will drive innovation in gadgets.

Comparative Analysis of SIM Technologies

How does iSIM compare to other SIM technologies?

Differences between eSIM and iSIM

Both eSIM and iSIM offer easy connectivity without physical SIM cards. But iSIM is built into the device's chip, giving better integration and security than eSIM.

Comparative Benefits of iSIM over Traditional SIM Cards

iSIM has many benefits over regular SIM cards. These include better security, lower costs, more efficiency, and stronger connectivity. With iSIM, makers can create smaller, integrated devices.

Industry-Wide Adoption of iSIM

The use of iSIM in different fields is growing fast. From healthcare to industrial IoT, iSIM's benefits, like security and efficiency, are speeding up its spread. Providers and markets are embracing iSIM for its future potential and innovative solutions.

Industry-Specific Applications

How is iSIM used in smart cities?

In smart cities, the use of iSIM technology is changing how we connect. Integrated SIMs (iSIMs) allow smooth communication between different IoT devices, making city management and infrastructure better. This helps with smart traffic control, efficient energy use, and advanced security systems. The iSIM ecosystem supports diverse applications, making urban living more connected and efficient.

iSIM in Industrial IoT

The industrial IoT sector gains a lot from iSIM integration. By putting iSIMs into IoT devices, industries get better connectivity and efficiency. This improves production monitoring, predictive maintenance, and resource management. iSIM deployment ensures reliable communication even in tough industrial settings, helping with advancements and operational success.

Role of iSIM in Retail and Asset Management

In retail and asset management, iSIM technology is transforming operations. iSIM cards help with real-time tracking and inventory management, aiding businesses in maintaining accurate stock levels and reducing losses. In asset management, iSIM applications provide better control and monitoring of valuable assets, ensuring their safety and optimizing use.

Challenges and Solutions in Deploying iSIM

What are the challenges in iSIM deployment?

Deploying iSIM technology comes with several challenges, like ensuring connectivity across different industries, including smartphones and wearables. iSIM adoption also needs strong security measures to prevent data breaches. Effective iSIM management and implementation strategies are key to overcoming these issues and ensuring smooth integration.

Solutions to Address iSIM Integration Challenges

To address iSIM integration challenges, several solutions can be used:

  • Better security protocols to protect data.
  • Simple provisioning processes for easier deployment.
  • Advanced connectivity solutions for different IoT applications.
  • Continuous monitoring and management to ensure optimal performance.

The Role of CommsCloud in iSIM Innovation

How does CommsCloud leverage iSIM for connectivity?

CommsCloud plays a key role in improving iSIM connectivity within the IoT and telecom sectors. By developing innovative iSIM solutions, CommsCloud enhances connectivity and promotes iSIM adoption. Their approach encourages greater integration and operational efficiencies across various technology platforms.

Benefits Provided by CommsCloud's iSIM Solutions

The benefits of CommsCloud's iSIM solutions include:

  • Secure and reliable connectivity for different IoT applications.
  • Increased efficiency in device management and communication.
  • Improved security measures to protect sensitive data.

CommsCloud's Contributions to iSIM Technology Development

CommsCloud contributes significantly to the growth and innovation of iSIM technology. Through dedicated research and advancements, CommsCloud drives the evolution of iSIM applications, supporting new trends and future potential within the IoT landscape.

 

Key Insights

  • An iSIM is the newest and smallest SIM form, built inside the cellular IC for better security and flexibility.
  • Learn about the differences between traditional SIMs, eSIMs, and iSIMs with our full explanations of their features and uses.
  • Understand how iSIM deployment is changing industries like automotive, IoT security, and mobile platforms with new opportunities and innovations.
  • Discover the role of key entities like EUICC, UICC, NB-IoT, and companies like Qualcomm Technologies in pushing forward iSIM technologies.
  • Explore the latest improvements in iSIM solutions, modules, and services that offer better performance, network compatibility, and data management.
  • Get insights into iSIM's impact on trending topics such as AIOT, 6G, Bluetooth 5, and the use of fingerprint sensors and chiplets in embedded devices.

title: Mastering Satellite IoT Connectivity
author: Joanne
content_text: August 30 | By Curtis Govan - floLIVE Satellite connectivity extends coverage to achieve global connectivity, a critical advancement for IoT devices. This technology complements cellular connectivity by utilizing satellite networks such as LEO (Low Earth Orbit) and GEO (Geostationary Equatorial Orbit), supporting new business use cases in rural or remote areas. Traditionally, consumer and business connectivity relied on terrestrial networks, primarily cellular networks. Although a significant portion of the global population has cellular access, vast geographic areas—like seas, deserts, and mountainous regions—remain uncovered. Satellite connectivity bridges these gaps, providing a robust backup or alternative to cellular networks for IoT devices. What are the benefits of using satellite connectivity? Historically, satellite systems were expensive last-resort options due to high costs and significant administrative overhead. Consider consumer applications like satellite phones, used on
